transcript.transcript: Hi, my name is HannaLore Hein and I joined the Junior League of Boise in January of 2016, I believe, and I went through the provisional experience, uh, that spring semester and was placed on the membership team for my first placed experience. Um, over time, I have served in a number of positions, including the fundraising chair, our nominations committee chair. Uh, I've served on membership. And I also went through the leadership roles to be president elect and president during the 2022 2023 league year. Uh, currently, I am an active member. I took a year off as a sabbatical kind of sustainer, catch my breath moment. And, uh, currently I'm serving on the communications chair and helping to advance the board's oral history initiative. How would I describe the Junior League as an organization? You know, I would describe the Junior League of Boise, uh, because again, we are one of nearly 300 leagues worldwide. But I think that our league really stands out as an organization that attracts like minded women who are willing to sacrifice of themselves for the benefit of others, uh, and that we have fun in the process. You know, the work that we've done as an organization during my tenure, uh, has certainly left a mark. And I think the impact that we can see on our community, although small, is measurable, um, because it's lasting. You look across this entire Treasure Valley, and so much of what exists today is a byproduct of the Junior League of Boise. And so I think that's really where, um, you see the mission shining through is in the work that's on the ground. Um, and, you know, we're we're humble. I think our culture, you know, community, organizational culture has a kind of through line of humility where we don't have to brag. We don't have to scream to the heavens about what the junior league has done or how it has helped us. But, uh, we let the work speak for itself. What inspired me to join? So my, uh, inspiration for joining was actually the league's history. I went to a fettuccine forum, uh, kind of public lecture that the city Department of Arts and History hosts. And in that presentation, I heard from then president, uh, Loren Paul talking about how this organization really helped shape her as a, as a leader and how integral it's been to, uh, you know, the development and growth of Boise and the Treasure Valley or more broadly. And so I think when I first heard about the organization, I was really, um, I had really high expectations of myself in terms of how how am I going to even find a place in this organization when it's already gone on and done all of these things? And I was young and I was new in my career, and I think I had a bit of imposter syndrome when it comes to, um, the expectations. And then I got in and realized, oh, this is what you make it. And so if I had high expectations for myself, um, I made the most of those, and I journeyed down the path of leadership to serve as president. And I'm so proud that I did. And I'm so grateful for all of the women who helped support me in that work, you know, especially, um, my presidential advisor Deborah Purdy, and everyone who served on the board with me when I was in that role. So I think that my experience, uh, exceeded even those high expectations that I had for myself because I learned so much in the process. A former president named Michelle Wooten counseled me when I first set myself up to be on that path, that you will be running a marathon at a sprinter's pace, and that you won't really understand the growth that you go through as an individual and as a leader until you're through it. Because you're just you're constantly pivoting, you're constantly innovating, you're constantly watching for how your work and how the work of your fellow leaders is going to impact others in the organization and in the community that we serve. So I would not have traded this in for anything, and I will be a member up until until I die. This is an organization I am committed to for life, because I've gotten so much out of it, and I see its potential every day. Share a meaningful project or moment that you participated in and why it stands out. You know, I think one of the most meaningful projects that I worked on was the work during our 90th year under Mariana Peavy's leadership to help us honor and, uh, tell our organization's history. So during our 90th year, we convened a committee of women, sustainers and actives and professionals that worked to archive and digitize a number of our historic records so that we had more nuanced stories to tell about what we've done in the past. And we made that information more accessible to the public and to our members at large. And I think that project really gave me the confidence that I needed in my professional life as a historian, uh, to ultimately step into the role that I currently hold, which is that of the Idaho State historian for the state of Idaho. Uh, how is my involvement in the league influenced my growth, leadership skills or community connections? Well, I feel like I've kind of answered this question in some of what I've already mentioned, but this organization has propelled me forward in ways that I did not even think were possible. I attribute the fact that I have been with the Idaho State Historical Society for six years, with increasing responsibility and opportunities for leadership because of what I learned through Junior League. And it was really the little things like, how do you write a agenda? How do you manage a meeting, how do you make time on the front end and the back end for connection with your fellow committee members or board members or, you know, stakeholders that you're working with in the community? And I didn't know how to do that. And yet I was working in an office setting that required professionalism in managing people's time and in bringing people together to collaborate and solve problems and advance projects. So that has really been critical to my growth as a woman in the workforce, as a leader in my own organization. And then I think with regard to the connections in the community. I mean, so many of us as league members also work for some really worthwhile organizations or were involved in other worthwhile things in the community. So I view those connections, um, as as important. From from what I've gained from within the organization as those relationships that I've, that I've cultivated externally, you know, including the work that we did with the Boise Art Museum during my tenure as president for project 100, uh, to the work that we did during 2020 and with the Idaho State Historical Society as part of the women 100 initiative, which celebrated women's suffrage and, uh, you know, a number of organizations in between. What challenges have I faced while working with the League and how did I overcome them? My biggest challenge, I think, comes down to understanding and gauging my own capacity. Um, I am the kind of person that will go all in on things, and that's great until you're all in, uh, results in you being burnt out. And so what I feel like I have learned through my time in the league is the importance of pacing yourself and the importance of striving for progress, not perfection. So one of the, I think, biggest takeaways that I have taken, and I employ this in my daily job too, is, um, the amount of prep time I will put into a meeting, or the amount of prep time I will put into a public presentation has to be in some sort of, you know, relation to the actual length of the meeting or the length of the presentation, and if I'm spending a week preparing for an hour long meeting, is is that perfection or is that progress? And so kind of kind of gauging that. Um, has certainly been a lesson. Uh, and then there's also been opportunity after opportunity to work on interpersonal skills, specifically conflict resolution, conflict mitigation, uh, trying to bring people with different views or perspectives or opinions onto the same page, understanding how much information you need to share out at what level within the organization you know. Do the board members need the same volume of detail and information about something as, say, a brand new provisional member? Um, and so that has been really, really useful for the league, but it's also something I carry over into my day job. What hopes or advice do I have for future members of the Junior League? Gosh, I more than anything hope that you have been able to cultivate the kinds of friendships that I have cultivated through Junior League of Boise. I look through my, you know, messages on my phone and consider the women who I talk with most regularly about life, and they are all women I have met through the Junior League of Boise, and I am forever grateful for having met them and having served with them and grown alongside them. And, you know, the the depths of some of those relationships are lifelong. Um, and others may not be that, but for right now, in this season of my life, they are really, really important to me. Lastly, what do I hope will be the legacy of the Junior League of Boise for its members and for the community in the years to come? You know, being to two years out, three years out from our centennial celebration is a major milestone. I cannot tell you as a, as a, you know, professional historian, the number of companies who do not hit this hit, this milestone, or the number of nonprofit organizations and the fact that we've done it and we've done it with such grace, uh, and with such consistency is remarkable. And I hope that we can continue to be consistent in our service to the community, even if that service has to shift and look different to reflect the changing workforce, the, you know, additional needs that are often placed on women in the home. Uh, now that we are all juggling wearing multiple hats at once. But I think consistency is really important. And as we think about what our project work is going to look like beyond our 100th year and what our recruitment efforts are going to look like beyond our 100th year. Knowing that we continually attract the right women at the right time, uh, I think speaks to the culture of the organization and our past leaders abilities to cultivate connections. So that's what I hope for the organization. And then, of course, for the community. I hope that we can continue to build on the legacy of the brick and mortar projects that we were known for, or maybe not known for. Uh, and of course, those are the big the big ones like the Discovery Center of Idaho and the work that we've done with Zoo Boise and the work that we've done with the historical society, and strengthening those relationships and partnerships so that internally, our organization knows that the significance of that work. But then also those other organizations who are now standalone nonprofits understand, appreciate, and value the work that we've done and look to us as resources for work that they need help with in the future.

lines.line: # Introduction and Journey in the Junior League

> The speaker introduces herself and outlines her history with the Junior League of Boise, starting from her initial joining in 2016. She details her progression through various roles, including membership, fundraising chair, nominations committee chair, and ultimately serving as president elect and president. After a brief sabbatical, she returned as an active member and currently serves as communications chair, contributing to the board's oral history initiative. This chapter establishes her deep and varied involvement in the organization.

metadata.title: Introduction and Journey in the Junior League
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself and outlines her history with the Junior League of Boise, starting from her initial joining in 2016. She details her progression through various roles, including membership, fundraising chair, nominations committee chair, and ultimately serving as president elect and president. After a brief sabbatical, she returned as an active member and currently serves as communications chair, contributing to the board's oral history initiative. This chapter establishes her deep and varied involvement in the organization.

lines.line: # Culture and Impact of the Junior League

> The speaker describes the Junior League of Boise as a unique organization that brings together like-minded women dedicated to serving others. She highlights the fun and camaraderie within the group, as well as the lasting and measurable impact the league has had on the Treasure Valley community. The culture of humility is emphasized, with the organization preferring to let its work speak for itself rather than seeking recognition.

metadata.title: Culture and Impact of the Junior League

She highlights the fun and camaraderie within the group, as well as the lasting and measurable impact the league has had on the Treasure Valley community. The culture of humility is emphasized, with the organization preferring to let its work speak for itself rather than seeking recognition.

lines.line: > The speaker recounts her initial inspiration to join the Junior League, which stemmed from learning about its history and hearing from a past president about its influence on leadership and community development. She describes her initial feelings of imposter syndrome and high expectations, but found that the organization allowed her to shape her own experience. Her journey led her to leadership roles, and she expresses gratitude for the support she received and pride in her accomplishments.

metadata.title: Inspiration and Expectations for Joining
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts her initial inspiration to join the Junior League, which stemmed from learning about its history and hearing from a past president about its influence on leadership and community development. She describes her initial feelings of imposter syndrome and high expectations, but found that the organization allowed her to shape her own experience. Her journey led her to leadership roles, and she expresses gratitude for the support she received and pride in her accomplishments.

lines.line: > The speaker highlights a significant project during the Junior League's 90th year, which involved archiving and digitizing historical records to make the organization's history more accessible. This collaborative effort included both sustainers and actives, and professionals, and helped the speaker gain confidence in her professional life as a historian. The experience contributed to her eventual role as Idaho State historian.

metadata.title: Honoring History: The 90th Year Initiative
metadata.synopsis: The speaker highlights a significant project during the Junior League's 90th year, which involved archiving and digitizing historical records to make the organization's history more accessible. This collaborative effort included both sustainers and actives, and professionals, and helped the speaker gain confidence in her professional life as a historian. The experience contributed to her eventual role as Idaho State historian.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on the profound impact the Junior League has had on her professional development, leadership abilities, and community relationships. She credits the organization with teaching her essential skills such as meeting management, collaboration, and professionalism, which have been critical in her career at the Idaho State Historical Society. The league also facilitated valuable connections with other organizations and community projects.

metadata.title: Personal Growth and Community Connections
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the profound impact the Junior League has had on her professional development, leadership abilities, and community relationships. She credits the organization with teaching her essential skills such as meeting management, collaboration, and professionalism, which have been critical in her career at the Idaho State Historical Society. The league also facilitated valuable connections with other organizations and community projects.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the challenges she faced, particularly around managing her own capacity and avoiding burnout. She learned the importance of pacing herself and striving for progress rather than perfection. The experience also provided opportunities to develop interpersonal skills, such as conflict resolution and effective communication, which have been beneficial both within the league and in her professional life.

metadata.title: Challenges and Lessons Learned
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the challenges she faced, particularly around managing her own capacity and avoiding burnout. She learned the importance of pacing herself and striving for progress rather than perfection. The experience also provided opportunities to develop interpersonal skills, such as conflict resolution and effective communication, which have been beneficial both within the league and in her professional life.

lines.line: # Advice and Hopes for Future Members

> The speaker shares her hopes for future members, emphasizing the value of friendships and relationships formed through the Junior League. She reflects on the importance of these connections in her life, noting that many of her closest friends are fellow league members. She expresses gratitude for the support and camaraderie she has experienced.

metadata.title: Advice and Hopes for Future Members
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares her hopes for future members, emphasizing the value of friendships and relationships formed through the Junior League. She reflects on the importance of these connections in her life, noting that many of her closest friends are fellow league members. She expresses gratitude for the support and camaraderie she has experienced.

lines.line: # Legacy and Future Vision for the Junior League

> The speaker looks ahead to the organization's centennial milestone, recognizing its rarity and significance. She hopes for continued consistency in service, adaptability to changing needs, and ongoing recruitment of dedicated women. She also wishes for the league to build on its legacy of impactful projects and partnerships, ensuring that both members and the broader community appreciate and value the organization's contributions.

story.description: The interview with HannaLore Hein highlights her experience and leadership roles within the Junior League of Boise since joining in 2016. She shares insights on the organization's impact, emphasizing its culture of humility and community service. Hein discusses significant projects like archiving the League's history, which bolstered her professional confidence. Her involvement has enhanced her leadership skills and community connections. She advises future members to foster friendships and hopes the League continues its consistent community service, adapting to contemporary demands, while maintaining its historical legacy and partnerships with local organizations.
story.title: November GMM - What does the JLB Mean to You
